title: Sergio Corbucci

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(October 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

(Italian: [[ˈsɛrdʒo korˈbuttʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 6 December 1926 – 1 December 1990) was an Italian [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter") and [producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer"). He was one of the main exponents of the [Spaghetti Western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") genre during the 1960s and 1970s,[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) with his most notable works including the original [*Django*](/wiki/Django_(1966_film) "Django (1966 film)"), *[Navajo Joe](/wiki/Navajo_Joe "Navajo Joe")*, *[The Great Silence](/wiki/The_Great_Silence "The Great Silence")*, [*The Mercenary*](/wiki/The_Mercenary_(film) "The Mercenary (film)"), and [*Compañeros*](/wiki/Compa%C3%B1eros_(film) "Compañeros (film)"). He also had a successful career directing comedies.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Italian film director (1926-1990)

| Sergio Corbucci | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | (1926-12-06)6 December 1926  [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") |
| Died | 1 December 1990(1990-12-01) (aged 63)  Rome, [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") |
| Other names | Stanley Corbett Gordon Wilson Jr. Enzo Corbucci |
| Occupation | Film director |
| Style | * [Spaghetti Western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") * [action comedy](/wiki/Action_comedy_film "Action comedy film") |
| Height | 1.77 m (5 ft 10 in) |
| Relatives | [Bruno Corbucci](/wiki/Bruno_Corbucci "Bruno Corbucci") (brother) Leonardo Corbucci (nephew) |

**Sergio Corbucci** (Italian: [[ˈsɛrdʒo korˈbuttʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 6 December 1926 – 1 December 1990) was an Italian [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter") and [producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer"). He was one of the main exponents of the [Spaghetti Western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") genre during the 1960s and 1970s,[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) with his most notable works including the original [*Django*](/wiki/Django_(1966_film) "Django (1966 film)"), *[Navajo Joe](/wiki/Navajo_Joe "Navajo Joe")*, *[The Great Silence](/wiki/The_Great_Silence "The Great Silence")*, [*The Mercenary*](/wiki/The_Mercenary_(film) "The Mercenary (film)"), and [*Compañeros*](/wiki/Compa%C3%B1eros_(film) "Compañeros (film)"). He also had a successful career directing comedies.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Corbucci is sometimes referred to as "the other Sergio", referring to fellow Spaghetti Western director [Sergio Leone](/wiki/Sergio_Leone "Sergio Leone").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Corbucci was born in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") in 1926. He had a younger brother [Bruno](/wiki/Bruno_Corbucci "Bruno Corbucci") (1931-1996), also a filmmaker.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He originally studied [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics") at university, before working as a film critic. For a period after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), he wrote for [*Stars and Stripes*](/wiki/Stars_and_Stripes_(newspaper) "Stars and Stripes (newspaper)").

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early work")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Sergio_Corbucci "Special:EditPage/Sergio Corbucci") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(October 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Corbucci made his directorial debut in 1951 with *[Salvate mia figlia](/wiki/Salvate_mia_figlia "Salvate mia figlia")*. His early works were mainly [melodramas](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama") and [crime films](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film"). Beginning with 1961's *[Goliath and the Vampires](/wiki/Goliath_and_the_Vampires "Goliath and the Vampires")*, he directed [sword and sandal](/wiki/Sword_and_sandal "Sword and sandal") movies. He also made several popular comedies, featuring the likes of [Totò](/wiki/Tot%C3%B2 "Totò") and [Franco and Ciccio](/wiki/Franco_and_Ciccio "Franco and Ciccio").

In 1963, he directed the ensemble war comedy *[The Shortest Day](/wiki/The_Shortest_Day "The Shortest Day")*, which was produced as a benefit film for the studio [Titanus](/wiki/Titanus "Titanus"). A parody of the Hollywood epic [*The Longest Day*](/wiki/The_Longest_Day_(film) "The Longest Day (film)"), the film featured an all-star cast of dozens of well-known performers, many of them in brief [cameo appearances](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance").

### Spaghetti Westerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spaghetti Westerns")]

Corbucci's first Westerns were the films *[Grand Canyon Massacre](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_Massacre "Grand Canyon Massacre")* (1964), which he co-directed (under the pseudonym Stanley Corbett) with [Albert Band](/wiki/Albert_Band "Albert Band"), as well as *[Minnesota Clay](/wiki/Minnesota_Clay "Minnesota Clay")* (1964), his first solo-directed spaghetti Western.

His biggest commercial success was with the cult spaghetti Western *[Django](/wiki/Django_(1966_film) "Django (1966 film)")*, starring [Franco Nero](/wiki/Franco_Nero "Franco Nero"), the leading man in many of his movies.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He would later collaborate with Nero on two other spaghetti Westerns, *[The Mercenary](/wiki/The_Mercenary_(film) "The Mercenary (film)")* (1968) — where Nero played Sergei Kowalski, a Polish mercenary and the film also starring [Tony Musante](/wiki/Tony_Musante "Tony Musante"), [Jack Palance](/wiki/Jack_Palance "Jack Palance") and [Giovanna Ralli](/wiki/Giovanna_Ralli "Giovanna Ralli") — as well as *[Compañeros](/wiki/Compa%C3%B1eros_(film) "Compañeros (film)")* (1970) — which also starred [Tomas Milian](/wiki/Tomas_Milian "Tomas Milian") and [Jack Palance](/wiki/Jack_Palance "Jack Palance"). The last film of the "Mexican Revolution" trilogy - *[The Mercenary](/wiki/The_Mercenary_(film) "The Mercenary (film)")* and *[Compañeros](/wiki/Compa%C3%B1eros_(film) "Compañeros (film)")* being the first two in the installment - was *[What Am I Doing in the Middle of the Revolution?](/wiki/What_Am_I_Doing_in_the_Middle_of_the_Revolution%3F "What Am I Doing in the Middle of the Revolution?")* (1972).

After *Django*, Corbucci made many other spaghetti Westerns, which made him the most successful Italian Western director after [Sergio Leone](/wiki/Sergio_Leone "Sergio Leone") and one of Italy's most productive and prolific directors.[[6]](#cite_note-6) His most famous of these pictures was *[The Great Silence](/wiki/The_Great_Silence "The Great Silence")* (Il Grande Silenzio), a dark and gruesome Western starring a [mute](/wiki/Speech_disorder "Speech disorder") action hero and a [psychopathic](/wiki/Psychopathic "Psychopathic") bad guy.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) The film was banned in some countries for its excessive violence.

Corbucci also directed *[Navajo Joe](/wiki/Navajo_Joe "Navajo Joe")* (1966), starring [Burt Reynolds](/wiki/Burt_Reynolds "Burt Reynolds") as the title character, a Navajo Indian opposing a group of bandits that killed his tribe, as well as *[The Hellbenders](/wiki/The_Hellbenders "The Hellbenders")* (1967), and *[Johnny Oro](/wiki/Johnny_Oro "Johnny Oro")* (1966) starring [Mark Damon](/wiki/Mark_Damon "Mark Damon"). Other spaghetti Westerns he directed include *[The Specialists](/wiki/Gli_specialisti "Gli specialisti")* (1969), with [Johnny Hallyday](/wiki/Johnny_Hallyday "Johnny Hallyday"); *[Sonny and Jed](/wiki/La_Banda_J.S.:_Cronaca_criminale_del_Far_West "La Banda J.S.: Cronaca criminale del Far West")* (1972), with Tomas Milian, [Susan George](/wiki/Susan_George_(actress) "Susan George (actress)") and [Telly Savalas](/wiki/Telly_Savalas "Telly Savalas"); and *[The White the Yellow and the Black](/wiki/The_White_the_Yellow_and_the_Black "The White the Yellow and the Black")* (1975), with Milian and [Eli Wallach](/wiki/Eli_Wallach "Eli Wallach").

Corbucci's Westerns were dark and brutal, with the characters portrayed as sadistic [antiheroes](/wiki/Antihero "Antihero"). His films featured very high body counts and scenes of mutilation. *Django* especially is considered to have set a new level for violence in Westerns.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later career")]

In the 1970s and 1980s, Corbucci mostly directed comedies, often starring [Adriano Celentano](/wiki/Adriano_Celentano "Adriano Celentano"). Many of these comedies were huge successes at the Italian box office and found wide distribution in European countries like Germany, France, Austria and Switzerland, but were not widely seen in English-speaking territories.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

He directed the [Terence Hill & Bud Spencer](/wiki/Terence_Hill_and_Bud_Spencer "Terence Hill and Bud Spencer") film *[Who Finds a Friend Finds a Treasure](/wiki/Who_Finds_a_Friend_Finds_a_Treasure "Who Finds a Friend Finds a Treasure")* (1981), as well as the solo Terence Hill vehicle *[Super Fuzz](/wiki/Super_Fuzz "Super Fuzz")*.

His last film was the action-drama *[Women in Arms](/wiki/Women_in_Arms "Women in Arms")* (1991), starring [Lina Sastri](/wiki/Lina_Sastri "Lina Sastri") and [Donald Pleasence](/wiki/Donald_Pleasence "Donald Pleasence").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Corbucci was married twice. His nephew is filmmaker Leonardo Corbucci.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

Corbucci died of a [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") at his home in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), five days before his 64th birthday, on December 1, 1990.[[12]](#cite_note-12) His remains were buried at a family plot in the [Campo Verano](/wiki/Campo_Verano "Campo Verano").

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Corbucci's Westerns were rarely taken seriously by contemporary critics[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) and he was considered an exploitation director, but he has managed to attain a cult reputation.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The website *Cinema Archives* ranked Corbucci as the 199th greatest director of all time.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 2021, was released a documentary about Corbucci, directed by Luca Rea, *[Django & Django](/wiki/Django_%26_Django "Django & Django")*, that relies to a considerable extent on an interview with Quentin Tarantino.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2022, German thrash metal band [Kreator](/wiki/Kreator "Kreator") released the instrumental song "Sergio Corbucci is Dead" as an intro to their album [Hate Über Alles](/wiki/Hate_%C3%9Cber_Alles "Hate Über Alles"). According to vocalist/guitarist [Mille Petrozza](/wiki/Mille_Petrozza "Mille Petrozza"), "Sergio Corbucci was someone who was very anti-authoritarian in his film. In all his films, he has a protagonist who rebels against the authorities. Often these characters are very obscure. I was wondering if there are still people like that who make really political films without trying to preach anything to you. It's a bit of a dig at the bands who don't speak their minds out of fear of losing fans."[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergio_Corbucci&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Actor")]
